stories.title: Bravo Property Trust Lends $68M on New Jersey Waterfront Apartments
stories.excerpt: Skyrock Capital has landed $68 million of construction financing for the development of a luxury waterfront multifamily project in Bayonne, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. Bravo Property Trust supplied the loan for Skyrock’s planned 180-unit Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk apartment development. Elan Goldberg, co-head of originations at Bravo, originated the debt. “Bayonne’s waterfront is attracting serious long-term investment, and we were proud to give this sponsor the certainty to build,” Aaron Krawitz, CEO of Bravo Property Trust, said in a statement. Skyrock, under the entity Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk, acquired the 2.3-acre waterfront site along Newark Bay at 219 West Fifth Street for $4.55 million in 2019, Jersey Digs previously reported. Two floors of the six-story building will be utilized for parking with the property featuring amenities that include a swimming pool and fitness center, according to Jersey Digs. Skyrock Capital did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bravo Property Trust funds $68M construction loan for Skyrock's 180-unit Bayonne, N.J. luxury waterfront project.
Skyrock acquired 2.3-acre Newark Bay site for $4.55M in 2019; development includes parking, pool, fitness.
Bayonne waterfront attracting institutional capital; signals confidence in North Jersey multifamily fundamentals.
stories.summary: Skyrock Capital secured $68 million in construction financing from Bravo Property Trust for the 180-unit Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk project on a 2.3-acre Newark Bay waterfront site acquired in 2019 for $4.55 million. The six-story building will feature two floors of parking, a swimming pool, and fitness center. The deal reflects institutional lender appetite and investor confidence in waterfront repositioning projects in the North Jersey market.
stories.impact: Waterfront development financing in Hudson County signals emerging investor interest in transforming industrial/underutilized sites near NYC; rising construction loan availability and $68M ticket size suggest cap rates and leverage remain workable for value-add development. Multifamily developers should monitor North Jersey waterfront entitlements and pre-development opportunities as comparable to Brooklyn/Hoboken trajectory.

stories.title: PNC Rebranding 82 FirstBank Colorado Branch Locations
stories.excerpt: PNC Bank paid $4.1 billion for FirstBank Holding Co. earlier this year. Now, PNC is taking down the FirstBank signs and replacing them with their own at 82 branches throughout Colorado. PNC now has 120 branches statewide. The Denver Business Journal reports FirstBank was the state’s largest until PNC took it over. PNC is the nation’s seventh-largest bank and is now Denver’s largest. As of June 2025, FirstBank had 12.33% of Colorado’s market share by deposits and PNC held 0.95%. After the combination, PNC ranks second behind Colorado market-share leader Wells Fargo’s 18.13%. In April, PNC revealed it would lay off 777 people. The jobs eliminated were said to be primarily administrative. In 2024, PNC announced a $2 billion coast-to-coast expansion of its branches. The post PNC Rebranding 82 FirstBank Colorado Branch Locations appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PNC paid $4.1B for FirstBank Holding Co., acquiring Colorado's largest bank by deposits.
PNC rebranding 82 FirstBank branches in Colorado; reduces First-Bank market share, consolidates banking infrastructure.
PNC layoffs 777 primarily administrative staff; signals cost rationalization and branch consolidation post-M&A.
stories.summary: PNC Bank acquired FirstBank Holding Co. for $4.1 billion, making PNC Denver's largest bank (now second statewide behind Wells Fargo at 18.1% market share). PNC is rebranding 82 FirstBank branches across Colorado and announced 777 job cuts, primarily administrative. The consolidation moves PNC from 0.95% to combined 12.33% Colorado market share and increases statewide branches to 120.
stories.impact: MINIMAL IMPACT. Banking M&A and branch consolidation affect CRE lending capacity and pricing but are not direct signals of multifamily demand, supply, or development fundamentals. Monitor: if PNC/FirstBank had multifamily construction lending programs, consolidation may reduce lending competition and tighten construction financing terms in Colorado markets including Denver, Boulder, and Fort Collins.

stories.title: Nuveen Buys Pair of Upper West Side Multifamily Properties for $75M
stories.excerpt: Nuveen Real Estate has acquired a two-building multifamily portfolio on Manhattan’s Upper West Side, Commercial Observer has learned. Nuveen bought the properties at 200 Manhattan Avenue and 133 West 104th Street, known as Manhattan Valley Apartments, from L+M Development Partners for $75 million, according to property records and a source with knowledge of the deal. Together, the properties span 143,551 square feet and include 126 federal Section 8 voucher units. The buildings are near the north end of Central Park between Central Park West and Amsterdam Avenue, about six blocks from each other. 200 Manhattan Avenue was built in 1890 and stands six stories tall with 181 units, while 133 West 104th Street is a seven-story building with 28 units that was built in 1950. Ariel Property Advisors’ Victor Sozio, Shimon Shkury and Remi Mandell arranged the sale on behalf of the seller, the firm announced. Ariel declined to comment further. Spokespeople for Nuveen and L+M did not immediately respond to requests for comment. News of the sale comes after a recent flurry of activity on the Upper West Side. Last month, Hawkins Way Capital bought a hotel turned student housing property at 117 West 70th Street from the American Musical and Dramatic Academy for $80 million. And in May, Benchmark Real Estate Group purchased a 90-unit, 15-story apartment building at 698 West End Avenue for $42 million, CO reported. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Nuveen acquires two Upper West Side buildings from L+M Development Partners for $75M; 126 Section 8 units.
Portfolio spans 143,551 SF across 200 Manhattan Avenue (1890, 181 units) and 133 West 104th (1950, 28 units).
Institutional buyer targeting stabilized, income-backed multifamily near Central Park; reflects value in preservation + subsidy.
stories.summary: Nuveen Real Estate acquired two Upper West Side multifamily buildings from L+M Development Partners for $75 million. The 209-unit portfolio (126 Section 8 voucher units) spans a 143,551-square-foot portfolio comprising a 1890 six-story building with 181 units and a 1950 seven-story building with 28 units, both located between Central Park West and Amsterdam Avenue, six blocks apart.
stories.impact: Upper West Side acquisition at $359K/unit signals institutional appetite for stabilized, low-leverage, federally-subsidized multifamily in Manhattan prime locations. Section 8 backing provides de-facto rent floors and demand certainty; $75M entry price reflects perceived defensibility over market-rate exposure during potential downturn. Developers should note: preservation and Section 8 conversion remain viable repositioning strategies in high-cost submarkets.

stories.title: Berkadia Hires C&W’s Blake Okland as Chief Revenue Officer
stories.excerpt: Blake Okland, who led Cushman & Wakefield’s multifamily capital markets platform from December 2023 to early 2026, has joined Berkadia as the firm’s chief revenue officer, Commercial Observer can first report. Okland’s tenure at Berkadia began this spring in a role focused on driving growth within the brokerage’s investment sales and mortgage banking platforms and working under Hilary Provinse, executive vice president of capital markets. He said the opportunity to team up with Provinse coupled with working in the multifamily sector with versatile capital markets offerings spurred his decision to ultimately join the 17-year-old firm. “One of our strengths is playing the long game, and in our business that is very important,” Okland told CO. “‘On the one hand you have a long-term view, and on the other hand you have the ability to be spontaneous and pivot because our market and our world is super dynamic, so I viewed those all as distinct advantages for the platform.” Okland, who is based in Charlotte, N.C., was previously appointed president of C&W’s multifamily capital markets in late 2023 while simultaneously serving as chief revenue officer at Greystone as part of the two firms’ strategic joint equity, debt and structured financing partnership. He also had a nearly two-decade run from 2004 to 2022 with Apartment Realty Advisors, which Newmark acquired in 2014. Provinse said Okland’s deep experience built up largely on the investment sales side of the brokerage business and her more capital markets background will complement each other well as they look to more fully integrate the two units. “It’s no longer a siloed business as our clients want advisory support both as they sell and market properties as well as when they finance, structure or recap properties,” Provinse said. “Berkadia is at the point where we had invested a lot in people and capabilities, both in debt and sales. So with our ability to tie those together under Blake’s leadershi
bullets: Blake Okland exits Cushman & Wakefield (multifamily head, Dec 2023–early 2026) to join Berkadia as CRO.
Okland's hire signals Berkadia scaling capital markets platform amid multifamily refinance cycle; reports to EVP Hilary Provinse.
Talent migration from C&W to Berkadia reflects consolidation competition in multifamily brokerage and lending advisory.
stories.summary: Blake Okland, who led Cushman & Wakefield's multifamily capital markets platform from December 2023 through early 2026, has joined Berkadia as Chief Revenue Officer, effective spring 2025. Reporting to EVP Hilary Provinse, Okland will drive growth in investment sales and mortgage banking for the 17-year-old firm. Okland is based in Charlotte, N.C.
stories.impact: MINIMAL DIRECT IMPACT. Talent migration reflects competitive dynamics in multifamily brokerage and capital markets but does not signal fundamental demand or market shift. Contextual note: Okland's hire during peak refinance cycle (2026–2027 maturity wall) suggests Berkadia investing in advisory and execution bandwidth to capture deal flow from stressed/maturing portfolios. Developers should view this as signal of expanding financing options and brokerage capacity.

stories.title: Keyah Lands $38M to Reposition Miami Beach Hotel Into a Starwood Brand
stories.excerpt: Keyah Real Estate Group has nabbed $38 million to reposition a Miami Beach hotel, which will be branded after Starwood’s Treehouse Hotels, property records show. The adaptive reuse project will combine hotels that date back to the 1940s: the former Haddon Hall Hotel at 1500 Collins Avenue and the Campton Apartments at 1509 and 1515 Washington Avenue in South Beach’s Art Deco district. Maxim Credit Group provided the financing, increasing a $15 million loan issued in 2024 by $23 million. The Aventura-based developer purchased the hotels, which sit on a 1.8-acre site, this year for an undisclosed amount. Last year, Miami Beach officials approved plans to combine the properties into a 262-room hotel, which is expected to open in 2029. “By reimagining these historic buildings through a modern lens, the property creates an experience that respects Miami Beach’s architectural heritage while still feeling fresh, relaxed, and unmistakably Treehouse,” Raul Leal, CEO of Starwood Hotels, said in a statement. The endeavor marks the first Miami Beach project since Barry Sternlich’s Starwood Capital Group regained the rights to the Starwood Hotels brand last year, after a decade under the ownership of Marriott International. Besides the Treehouse name, the entity includes 1 Hotels and Baccarat brands. The financing comes as another hotel on Washington Avenue has struggled. Following a $205 million foreclosure judgment in May, CIM Group gained control of the 266-room Goodtime Hotel last week with a paltry $100 bid, The Real Deal reported. A representative for Keyah Real Estate Group did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Keyah lands $38M (additional $23M on existing $15M) from Maxim Credit to reposition Miami Beach hotel to Treehouse brand.
Adaptive reuse combines 1940s-era Haddon Hall Hotel (1500 Collins) and Campton Apartments (1509/1515 Washington).
262-room hotel opening 2029 on 1.8-acre South Beach Art Deco site; signals hotel capital + hotel-to-residential risk.
stories.summary: Keyah Real Estate Group secured $38 million in financing (a $23 million increase to an existing $15 million loan from Maxim Credit Group) for an adaptive reuse project combining historic hotels (dating to 1940s) in Miami Beach's Art Deco district. The development will yield a 262-room Treehouse-branded hotel on a 1.8-acre site, combining the Haddon Hall Hotel and Campton Apartments, expected to open in 2029.
stories.impact: Hotel repositioning capital flows signal investor confidence in Miami Beach tourism/hospitality recovery but are not direct multifamily demand signals. Relevance: strong hotel financing environment may compete for South Beach development capital and attract capital away from multifamily. Developers should monitor: if Treehouse hotel underperforms, site may revert to multifamily consideration; conversely, hospitality strength in Miami Beach signals population/tourist demand that can cross-support multifamily demand in adjacent/overflow markets.

stories.title: Beyond the Maturity Wall: Why Multifamily’s Next Refinance Cycle Looks More Workable Than the Headlines Suggest
stories.excerpt: The multifamily maturity wall has been a looming concern for several years. Through the rest of 2026 and into 2027, borrowers will be working through loans that were originated in a very different rate environment; often with leverage, pricing, and underwriting assumptions that today’s market will not fully support. At the same time, the financing landscape has become materially more open, competitive and flexible than it was a year ago. Capital solutions now are more varied than when rates first moved higher, although many now require greater structure and additional sponsor equity than in prior cycles. For sponsors who engage early, the current environment presents an opportunity to recapitalize proactively rather than reactively. The Story Behind the Numbers The size of the maturity wall tells only part of the story. Equally important is its composition. The Mortgage Bankers Association estimates that $875 billion of the $5 trillion in outstanding CRE loans is due to mature in 2026, followed by another $652 billion in 2027. But these maturities are far from evenly distributed across asset and lender types. Depositories, CMBS/CLO/ABS lenders, credit companies, and warehouse lenders account for roughly 75% of 2026 CRE maturities. Agency maturities are more heavily concentrated in the later years of the cycle, with the peak hitting in 2029-2030. Within multifamily specifically, banks hold 24% of debt maturing between 2025 and 2033, with 43% of those maturities coming due through 2027. Debt fund maturities show a similar front-loading, with 21% of their maturities concentrated in the near term compared with 18% for the overall market. The takeaway is that current refinancing stress is not evenly distributed across the multifamily market, and the issue is often less about asset viability and more about whether the existing capital stack still aligns with current lending standards. Capital is Available, but Disciplined The refinancing environment is materially better t
bullets: $875B of $5T CRE loans mature in 2026; $652B due in 2027; composition varies by lender and asset class.
Financing environment materially more open, competitive; capital solutions now require greater equity and structure than prior cycles.
Sponsors engaging early can recapitalize proactively; maturity wall size masks composition differences across depositories, CMBS, CLO, ABS, credit companies.
stories.summary: The CRE maturity wall is significant but navigable: $875 billion of the $5 trillion in outstanding CRE loans mature in 2026, followed by $652 billion in 2027. While headlines emphasize the absolute volume, the financing landscape has materially improved compared to one year ago—capital is more varied and competitive, though it requires greater sponsor equity and structural complexity. The composition of maturities is unevenly distributed across lenders and asset types, favoring sponsors who engage early in refinancing.
stories.impact: Critical for multifamily developers and owners: the maturity wall is real but refinancing capacity is expanding. Sponsors with stabilized assets, strong operations, and willingness to accept equity requirements can refinance proactively rather than face distress. Developers should prepare acquisition targets for 2026–2027: distressed portfolios will likely create pipeline opportunities as weak sponsors fail to refinance. Conversely, strong balance sheets and early engagement are now competitive moats.

stories.title: Newmark Lands Listings to Sell 3 Phoenix-Area Class A Office Properties
stories.excerpt: Newmark has the listings for three Class A office properties in the Phoenix Metro area. The Phoenix Business Journal reports the offices, totaling 545,000 square feet, are situated in Chandler, Scottsdale and Phoenix. Newmark brokers Barry Gabel, Chris Marchildon and CJ Osbrink are marketing the properties, which include: • One Chandler Corporate Center at 350 N. McClintock Drive is a two-story, 117,394-square-foot office that was built in 2018. The building is fully leased. The property was bought in 2019 by an entity connected to Strategic Office Partners for $25.2 million. • Northsight Corporate Center, at 14400 N. 87th St. in the Scottsdale Airpark. Vertical Ventures acquired the Scottsdale property for $42.25 million in 2024 from CIM Group. Vanguard has a lease at the center and expects to invest millions more in upgrades. • The third Newmark listing is The Summit (shown), a two-building, 280,904-square-foot office complex in Phoenix’s Piestewa Peak Corridor. Newmark is calling for offers on The Summit July 15. The post Newmark Lands Listings to Sell 3 Phoenix-Area Class A Office Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Newmark marketing three Phoenix-area Class A office buildings totaling 545K SF; Chandler, Scottsdale, Phoenix locations.
One Chandler Corporate Center (117K SF, 2018, full lease) bought 2019 for $25.2M; Summit (281K SF, two buildings) calling for offers.
Phoenix office selling signals landlord repositioning; office supply overhang drives pricing pressure and possible asset sales.
stories.summary: Newmark has listings for three Class A office properties in the Phoenix metro area totaling 545,000 square feet: One Chandler Corporate Center (117,394 SF, fully leased, 2018 build, acquired 2019 for $25.2M), Northsight Corporate Center in Scottsdale Airpark (acquired by Vertical Ventures for $42.25M in 2024), and The Summit, a 280,904-SF two-building complex in Phoenix's Piestewa Peak Corridor. Newmark is accepting offers on The Summit as of July 15.
stories.impact: Phoenix office supply overhang and landlord sales pressure signal potential cap rate expansion and valuation pressure on trophy office assets. Multifamily developers should monitor: office market softness may signal broader Phoenix economic cooling or that high-quality office tenants (Vanguard at Northsight) are relocating or consolidating, which could reduce demand for downtown/office-adjacent multifamily. Conversely, office weakness may free up capital and boost landlord appetite for multifamily conversion projects in Phoenix CBD submarkets.

stories.title: PwC Plans Relocation from DTLA to Century City in 2028
stories.excerpt: Longtime Downtown Los Angeles office tenant PwC is relocating to Century City. The Los Angeles Times reported that the accounting and professional services firm has signed a 15-year lease to occupy 150,000 square feet at Irvine Company’s 2121 Ave. of the Stars. Its current DTLA space at 601 S. Figueroa St. spans about 114,000 square feet. “This move is about growth, con­nectiv­ity, and the future of our Los Angeles busi­ness,” PwC office man­aging part­ner Andy Sofield told The Times. “PwC has con­tin­ued to grow in the mar­ket, and the new Cen­tury City office gives us addi­tional space in a loc­a­tion that bet­ter sup­ports our people, cli­ents, and teams across the region.” The Times reported that the PwC relocation, scheduled for 2028 upon the expiration of its 601 S. Figueroa lease, represents the latest high-­pro­file exit from the city’s fin­an­cial core. PwC has been a presence downtown since the late 1990s. The post PwC Plans Relocation from DTLA to Century City in 2028 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PwC relocating DTLA headquarters to Century City; 15-year lease at Irvine Co.'s 2121 Ave of Stars; move in 2028.
PwC expanding: 150K SF Century City (vs. 114K SF DTLA); represents major office tenant exit from downtown Los Angeles.
Downtown LA financial core losing marquee tenant; signals ongoing DTLA CBD demand deterioration and West Side flight.
stories.summary: PwC, a longtime Downtown Los Angeles office tenant, is relocating to Century City, signing a 15-year lease for 150,000 square feet at Irvine Company's 2121 Avenue of the Stars. The move, scheduled for 2028 upon lease expiration at its current 114,000-square-foot DTLA space (601 S. Figueroa), reflects the firm's growth and represents another high-profile exit from downtown's financial core. PwC has been in DTLA since the late 1990s.
stories.impact: PwC's departure signals accelerating downtown Los Angeles CBD decline and is a major demand headwind for DTLA multifamily. Loss of a marquee employer and high-wage tenant reduces residential demand and rent support in downtown. Conversely, Century City/West LA office strength may boost multifamily demand in proximity to Century City, Pico Boulevard, and Westside markets. Developers in DTLA should reassess assumptions on office worker population and household formation; downtown repositioning (office-to-residential conversion) may become more attractive as office demand deteriorates.

stories.title: Commercial Real Estate Needs a Company Brain, Not a Bigger AI Model
stories.excerpt: Every quarter, the same scene repeats across institutional real estate. A firm signs an enterprise AI deal or rolls out a company-wide subscription to the latest model. The pitch promises automated underwriting, instant lease abstraction and clean data. A big check goes out the door. Six months later, the results are underwhelming. The bill is bigger than expected, and most of the team is still doing the real work in Excel. Ninety-five percent of organizations are getting zero return on generative AI, despite $30 billion to $40 billion in enterprise investment, according to MIT’s 2025 State of AI in Business report. Arunabh Dastidar. Photo: Courtesy Leni. The usual explanations blame the technology or the people using it, but both miss the point. The real bottleneck is context. Until a firm solves that, no model will change the outcome, no matter how powerful it is. The reason projects fail is the fact that the tools cannot retain memory, don’t adapt to specific workflows, and fail to improve based on user feedback. In plain terms, a generic chatbot forgets what you told it last week, never learns how your firm works, and never gets better. That is why tools with no memory of your business stall out while those that build up that memory stick. Hence the winners are not the ones with the flashiest technology, but the ones that “embedded themselves inside workflows, adapting to context,” according to the MIT study. Anyone can scan a lease — the documents are not the hard part — but the real knowledge is in how experienced people read those documents to make decisions. That interpretive layer is the company brain. It gets captured over thousands of real deals, tested against the mistakes that actually show up in commercial leases, and sharpened through feedback in a live environment. The history is the asset, and that history is made of decisions far more than documents. In other words, adoption does not track how big the model is. It tracks how much
bullets: MIT study: 95% of orgs gaining zero ROI on generative AI despite $30–40B enterprise investment; context is the bottleneck.
Real estate firms signing big AI deals expecting automation; results underwhelming after 6 months, bills higher than expected.
Generic AI tools lack memory, workflow adaptation, and user learning; enterprise AI adoption failing due to lack of firm-specific context.
stories.summary: Despite $30–40 billion in enterprise AI investment, 95% of organizations are achieving zero return on investment, according to MIT's 2025 State of AI in Business report. Real estate firms continue signing major AI contracts promising automated underwriting and lease abstraction, but results disappoint within six months. The core issue is context: generic AI tools cannot retain institutional memory, adapt to firm workflows, or improve based on user feedback, making even powerful models ineffective without firm-specific training and data.
stories.impact: MINIMAL IMPACT ON MULTIFAMILY FUNDAMENTALS. This is a cautionary note on enterprise tech ROI, not a market signal. Contextual relevance: developers and capital teams evaluating AI-powered underwriting, market analysis, and transaction tools should demand firm-specific implementations and realistic ROI timelines. Technology adoption is not a free productivity win; expect integration costs and extended payback periods.

stories.title: JVP Begins Work on 110K-SF Office Building at Frisco’s The Mix
stories.excerpt: JVP Development broke ground on the first office building at The Mix (shown), a mixed-use development located in Frisco’s North Platinum Corridor. The 110,000-square-foot Class A office building, 7725 Lebanon, marks a major milestone for the development, where 110,000 square feet of retail space, 635 multifamily units and the first three acres of The Mix’s “Central Park” are already under construction. JLL’s T.D. Briggs and Blake Shipley are handling office leasing for the building on behalf of landlord JVP Development. 7725 Lebanon Road is expected to deliver in summer 2027. Arcadis is serving as architect, and AECOM Hunt is the general contractor for the office building. Located at the corner of Lebanon Road and Parkwood Boulevard, The Mix will feature more than 20 food and beverage options, two hotels, significant corporate headquarters and boutique office space, a nine-acre activated park and a variety of residential options. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post JVP Begins Work on 110K-SF Office Building at Frisco’s The Mix appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JVP Development breaks ground on 110K-SF Class A office at The Mix, Frisco North Platinum Corridor; delivers summer 2027.
Master-planned development includes 635 multifamily units, 110K SF retail, three-acre Central Park, two hotels under construction.
Frisco North Platinum office + multifamily + retail + hospitality; signals strong demand for mixed-use development in Dallas North Corridor.
stories.summary: JVP Development broke ground on the first office building at The Mix, a mixed-use development in Frisco's North Platinum Corridor. The 110,000-square-foot Class A office building (7725 Lebanon) will deliver in summer 2027. The broader master plan includes 635 multifamily units, 110,000 square feet of retail, 20+ food and beverage options, two hotels, a nine-acre activated park, and significant corporate headquarters space, already under construction.
stories.impact: The Mix represents strong institutional developer confidence in Frisco's North Platinum Corridor for master-planned, mixed-use density. The 635-unit multifamily component plus office/retail/hospitality signals market belief in employment density and walkable urban form in North Dallas. Multifamily developers should monitor: office leasing pace at 7725 Lebanon; strong office absorption would validate residential demand and support rent growth. Frisco supply pipeline is substantial; developers need to track competitive deliveries and pre-lease velocity at The Mix to assess absorption risk.

stories.title: Brooklyn Village site in Charlotte changes hands twice in two month span
stories.excerpt: An integral part of uptown Charlotte’s Brooklyn Village redevelopment was sold to a local Charlotte group, the second time in two months the property changed hands.  Charlotte-based First and Early Partners dropped $24.3 million to acquire 700 and 800 East Brooklyn Village Avenue from Peachtree Group, who acquired the deed from Peebles Corporation in May. In 2023, Peachtree greenlit $23.8 million in funds to New York-based Peebles to secure the location and fund construction, according to the Charlotte Business Journal.  Peachtree initially filed for foreclosure on the deed in early May, but voluntarily dismissed the action before the May 21 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: First & Early Partners acquire 700 and 800 East Brooklyn Village Avenue, Charlotte, for $24.3M from Peachtree Group.
Peachtree acquired property May 2025 from Peebles Corp (who got $23.8M funding in 2023); second deed transfer in two months.
5.8-acre site core to $700M Brooklyn Village redevelopment; original Phase 1 plan: 496 market-rate + 56 AMI multifamily + 20K SF retail.
stories.summary: Charlotte-based First & Early Partners acquired 700 and 800 East Brooklyn Village Avenue for $24.3 million from Peachtree Group, which had acquired the deed from Peebles Corporation in May 2025. The 5.8-acre site is central to the $700 million Brooklyn Village redevelopment; the original Phase 1 plan called for 496 market-rate multifamily units, 56 affordable units (60% AMI), and 20,000 square feet of retail. The rapid deed transfer (Peachtree acquired in May, First & Early in June) signals financial distress and sponsor transition.
stories.impact: Brooklyn Village project distress signals: rapid ownership change, foreclosure filing (Peachtree), and local buyer acquisition suggest original Peebles-led master plan may be compromised or restructured. Charlotte developers should monitor: First & Early's revised scope (not yet announced), timeline, and financing appetite for the massive 5.8-acre repositioning. If Phase 1 is reduced or delayed, it signals market resistance to original density/pricing; if First & Early re-scopes and accelerates, it signals acquisition of value-add opportunity. Uptown Charlotte multifamily supply pipeline is dependent on Brooklyn Village deliveries; uncertainty here creates planning risk for competing projects.

stories.title: Azorim secures financing for final phase of Yonkers complex
stories.excerpt: An Israel-based development firm is preparing to put the finishing touches on a massive multifamily complex a dozen miles north of Manhattan. Azorim landed $69 million in construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for the fourth and final building of Mizora at Ridge Hill in Yonkers, the Commercial Observer reported. This component of the master-planned community in the Westchester County city is at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard. The financing will support work on a 14-story, 174-unit building, dubbed Mizora Tower 4 (Mizora is “Azorim” spelled backward). There will also be 248 parking spaces as part of this phase of the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Azorim secures $69M construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for 174-unit Phase 4 at Mizora at Ridge Hill, Yonkers.
Mizora Tower 4 at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard is 14-story, 174 units, 248 parking spaces; part of 520-unit master plan.
Completed buildings: Phase 1 (~2014), Phase 2 (2023), Phase 3 (2024); Phase 4 delivers completion of phased Yonkers complex.
stories.summary: Israeli developer Azorim secured $69 million in fixed-rate, interest-only construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for Mizora Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit building at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard in Yonkers. The building will include 248 parking spaces and is the fourth and final phase of the Mizora at Ridge Hill master-planned community. Walker & Dunlop arranged the financing. When completed, the complex will total 520 units across four buildings, connected by a 25,000-square-foot amenity center.
stories.impact: Azorim's ability to finance Phase 4 ($69M) and prior Acore Capital loan (Phase 2–3, $145M, Fall 2024) signals: (1) lender confidence in Westchester County multifamily and Azorim as sponsor; (2) PILOT/market-rate bond structures enabling competitive financing despite 'ongoing market headwinds' (per lender quote); (3) successful lease-up/operation of prior phases justifying Phase 4 takeout. Yonkers supply of 520 units across Mizora plus 250-unit Hudson Street TOD signals Westchester multifamily growth. Developers should monitor Mizora absorption and rent trends; strong Yonkers performance could justify additional competitive entries north of NYC.

stories.title: Avery Hall plots 250 units at stalled Crown Heights hotel site
stories.excerpt: One year on from purchasing the site out of bankruptcy, Avery Hall Investments is revealing the future for a Crown Heights lot once envisioned for a hotel. The Brooklyn-based developer filed an application with the New York City Department of Buildings for a mixed-use project on a vacant parcel at 1550 Bedford Avenue, the Commercial Observer reported. Through its land use application, Avery Hall is petitioning for zoning amendments to permit a mix of residential and commercial use, along with a mandatory inclusionary housing area designation for affordable housing. Avery Hall plans to build a 173,000-square-foot building with 250 units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Avery Hall Investments files DOB application for 250-unit mixed-use at 1550 Bedford Avenue, Crown Heights Brooklyn.
18-story, 173K SF building: 250 units (75 affordable, MIH-designated); retail space; requires zoning amendments and commercial-to-residential conversion.
Avery Hall purchased site out of bankruptcy Aug 2024 for $10.1M (vs. original $14M valuation, $7M 2014 purchase by Goldman).
stories.summary: Avery Hall Investments is pursuing a mixed-use redevelopment at 1550 Bedford Avenue in Crown Heights, filing a NYC Department of Buildings application for an 18-story building with 250 units (75 designated affordable under Mandatory Inclusionary Housing), retail space, and 173,000 square feet total. The site was purchased out of bankruptcy in August 2024 for $10.1 million; it was originally acquired by Yoel Goldman in 2014 for $7 million and designated for a 100-key luxury hotel before funding dried up during the pandemic. The current zoning permits commercial and hotel use; Avery Hall is petitioning for amendments to allow residential and commercial.
stories.impact: Crown Heights rezoning and residential repositioning signals Brooklyn developer appetite for converting stalled commercial/hotel sites into multifamily. Avery Hall's 75-unit affordable component (30% of 250) reflects MIH requirements; strong developer interest in absorption despite affordability mandate suggests market-rate rent support compensates. Bankruptcy acquisition at steep discount ($10.1M vs. $14M valuation) illustrates distressed acquisition opportunity. Brooklyn developers should monitor: if Avery Hall's 250-unit Crown Heights project obtains approval and leases strongly, it validates MIH + market-rate mixed-income models in transitional Brooklyn neighborhoods. Zoning/entitlement risk remains; MIH rent reductions will compress returns.

stories.title: Abbott’s shift into reverse puts data center debate into overdrive
stories.excerpt: Texas’ typically pro-business governor did an about-face on data centers amid growing public sentiment against the resource-intensive developments.  Less than a year ago, Gov. Greg Abbott lauded Texas as “the epicenter of AI development” when Google announced a $40 billion investment in November, the Texas Tribune reported.  He changed his tune this week when he called for an outright ban on data centers in rural areas during a campaign stop in Bullard, per the outlet. The announcement built on a letter Abbott sent to the Texas Legislature in June when he called for regulating data center development in Texas. Specifically, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas Gov. Greg Abbott calls for rural data center ban; reverses prior AI/data center support (Google $40B investment Nov 2024).
Abbott previously lauded Texas as 'epicenter of AI'; now proposes annual reporting, limits incentives, close-loop water requirements.
San Marcos (first TX city) bans data centers; State Sen. Bettencourt vows legal challenge under HB 2559; data center construction $50B+ nationally.
stories.summary: Texas Governor Greg Abbott reversed his pro-data-center stance within less than a year, calling for an outright ban on data centers in rural areas. Previously, Abbott lauded Texas as the 'epicenter of AI development' following Google's $40 billion announcement in November 2024. Abbott's current proposals include annual data center reporting, limits on incentives, and mandatory close-loop water systems. San Marcos became the first Texas city to attempt a ban; State Senator Paul Bettencourt vowed to challenge it under House Bill 2559, which restricts municipalities' ability to restrict development.
stories.impact: Data center regulatory reversal signals growing anti-development/environmental backlash in Texas; could dampen growth trajectory in rural/peripheral Texas markets if bans spread. Immediate multifamily implications: (1) reduced property tax revenue for counties/municipalities if data center projects stall or relocate, shrinking public investment in schools/infrastructure and reducing housing demand; (2) conversely, if data center bans hold, rural sprawl pressure eases, potentially supporting in-fill multifamily in established Texas metros (Austin, Dallas, Houston, San Antonio). Monitor: if data centers are banned statewide, AI/tech job growth may concentrate in urban cores, supporting urban multifamily submarkets. If bans are struck down legally (likely under HB 2559), rural infrastructure/environmental degradation could accelerate, fragmenting development patterns.
